{{Chess Opening Theory/Position|= |King's Indian Attack| |rd|nd|bd|qd|kd|bd|nd|rd|= |pd|pd|pd|pd| |pd|pd|pd|= | | | | |pd| | | |= | | | | | | | | |= | | | | |pl| | | |= | | | |pl| | | | |= |pl|pl|pl| | |pl|pl|pl|= |rl|nl|bl|ql|kl|bl|nl|rl|= || }} =King's Indian Attack= This is a way for White to avoid the "classical" French Defence. Though White's game looks less active (White will have to fianchetto his king's bishop, leaving time for Black to develop), a powerful attack is possible by advancing the king's pawn to e5, building a strong outpost. Like in a [[Chess/Sicilian Defence|Sicilian defence]], White will launch a king-side attack, and Black can counter-attack at the queen side. The main continuation is [[/2...d5/|d5]], though [[/2...c5/|c5]] is also correct (many transpositions exist in the King's Indian). =Theory Table= {{Chess Opening Theory/Table}}. '''1. e4 e6 2. d3'''
